column1: This is the last name of the person (human) adding the entry.
column2: Add the name of the AI agent, along with the model version.
column4: List all authors in the paper.
column7: This refers to the type of published article.  Use keywords "research", "review", "perspective", or similar.
column9: This refers to the mathematical modeling framework considered in the article.  Use keywords "Boolean network", "ODE", "graph based", “stochastic”, or similar.  Multiple keywords are permitted.
column10:
Consider both mathematical and biological descriptions included in the paper.  Some aspects to include:
+ directed or undirected?
+ hierarchical?
+ can modules share elements/intersect?
+ can modules be nested?
+ Does the module definition include structural components?  Such as is the definition graph based?
+ Does the module definition include functional components?  Such as is the definition function based?
column11: This refers to the main context in which a module is defined.  Briefly describe the biological setting and intended use of the module concept, such as evolved vs engineered system, gene regulatory network vs signaling network, and whether the purpose is analysis, control, design, validation, etc.
column12: What is the motivation for the definition of a module in the paper?  What biological phenomena is being captured by this definition?
column13: This refers to whether there is an algorithm or implementation to the proposed definition of a module.  Use keyword "yes" or "no".  If "yes", add brief description of the algorithm(s).
column14: This refers to if data is included in the paper.  State whether the paper includes data, and if so, what kind of data and how it is used. Distinguish empirical, simulated, benchmark, or literature-curated data when possible.
column15: This refers to biological levels.  Use keywords "cell", "tissue", or similar.
column16: Use the convention C. elegans, S. cerevisiae, etc.
column17:
This refers to the impact of the work with justification. Do not limit to only citation count.  Questions to consider.
+ Is there broad use of the work?
+ How impactful is the work up to now?
+ Are there follow-up papers?
+ In what subdiscipline(s) is this work impactful?
column18: Add the number of citations for this paper.
column19: Give a short (1-2 sentences) summary of the paper, appropriate for an annotated bibliography.

column4: Sergio Antonio Alcalá-Corona, Santiago Sandoval-Motta, Jesús Espinal-Enríquez, Enrique Hernández-Lemus
column5: Modularity in Biological Networks
column9: Graph-based / community detection / network analysis (multiple methods reviewed)
column10: Modules are groups of biological entities (genes, proteins, metabolites) more densely interconnected with each other than with the rest of the network. The review surveys structural definitions (community structure), functional definitions (shared biological function), and their relationship. Different algorithms operationalize different aspects of this general definition. Modules may be hierarchical and overlapping depending on the method used.
column11: Gene regulatory networks, protein interaction networks, metabolic networks, and co-expression networks. The review is comprehensive and applicable to any type of biological network.
column12: To provide a comprehensive survey of modularity concepts, detection algorithms, and biological significance across multiple network types.
column13: No; review paper comparing existing algorithms and their applications.
column14: No new experimental data; reviews published network datasets and algorithmic approaches.
column17: Moderate to high impact. Comprehensive contemporary review useful as a one-stop reference for the current state of modularity analysis across biological network types.
column19: This comprehensive review surveys the concept of modularity across diverse biological network types — gene regulatory, protein interaction, metabolic, co-expression — comparing algorithmic approaches and their biological interpretations.

column4: Uri Alon
column5: Network motifs: theory and experimental approaches
column9: Graph-based regulatory networks; randomized-network motif detection; dynamical models of transcription circuits
column10: The paper treats modules as network motifs: recurring regulation patterns or circuits that occur much more often than expected in randomized networks and can be viewed as basic building blocks of transcription networks. These motifs are directed subgraphs identified structurally but interpreted functionally, and they can overlap and combine into larger organizations such as multi-output feedforward loops, SIMs, DORs, feedback loops, and cascades.
column11: Primarily evolved transcription regulation networks in sensory and developmental contexts, with extensions to signaling and neuronal networks. Motifs are used to relate network topology to information-processing function using interaction maps and dynamic experiments.
column12: To identify basic circuit building blocks in biological regulation and explain how recurrent motif structure supports specific information-processing tasks such as delay, pulse generation, response acceleration, memory, and temporal ordering.
column13: Yes; network motifs are detected by comparing subgraph frequencies in observed networks against randomized networks, and their functions are analyzed with mathematical models and tested experimentally.
column14: Uses regulatory-network topology, comparative network data across organisms, fluorescent reporter time-series, and microarray-based transcription dynamics to identify and validate motif functions.
column16: multiple (E. coli, S. cerevisiae, B. subtilis, plants, animals)
column17: Highly influential review that consolidated network motif theory with experimental validation and helped shape systems biology, transcription-network analysis, and synthetic gene circuit design.
column19: This review synthesizes theory and experiments on network motifs as recurring circuit patterns in transcription networks. It argues that motifs are basic building blocks with characteristic information-processing functions and surveys how they are identified, modeled, and validated across diverse organisms.
column20: A.2 Structural Definitions: Network Motifs as Elementary Modules

column4: Ido Amit; Ami Citri; Tal Shay; Yiling Lu; Menachem Katz; Fan Zhang; Gabi Tarcic; Doris Siwak; John Lahad; Jasmine Jacob-Hirsch; Ninette Amariglio; Nora Vaisman; Eran Segal; Gideon Rechavi; Uri Alon; Gordon B. Mills; Eytan Domany; Yosef Yarden
column5: A module of negative feedback regulators defines growth factor signaling
column9: Signaling network / kinetic systems analysis / network motifs
column10: A module is a kinetically defined, coexpressed cluster of delayed early genes that functions as a negative-feedback unit attenuating growth factor signaling. In this paper, the module is defined primarily by shared temporal behavior and shared regulatory function rather than by a purely graph-theoretic partition. The authors also interpret the signaling network through smaller architectural motifs such as negative autoregulation and coherent/incoherent feed-forward loops.
column11: Mammalian growth-factor signaling, especially EGFR signaling in epithelial cells (HeLa and MCF10A). The module is embedded in a signaling-transcription network and is treated as a conserved systems-level architecture for pathway attenuation and temporal control.
column12: To explain how growth-factor responses are made transient and robust by transcription-dependent feedback, and to identify the regulators that delimit the temporal window of signaling and transcription.
column13: yes - time-course clustering/order by peak expression time, reverse-phase protein lysate array and microarray profiling, interaction-map construction, tumor-compendium/module analysis, and motif-based network interpretation
column14: Time-series phosphoprotein data (RPPA), gene-expression microarrays, qPCR, luciferase assays, immunoprecipitation, siRNA perturbations, and human tumor expression compendia with outcome data.
column17: High impact in systems biology and signal transduction: it extended network-motif reasoning to mammalian growth-factor signaling, identified a delayed-early negative-feedback module, and linked loss of this module to cancer phenotypes and prognosis.
column19: The paper shows that EGFR signaling induces waves of transcription that include a delayed early cluster of negative regulators. The authors argue that this cluster forms a structured module that attenuates MAPK signaling and immediate-early transcription, and they interpret the response using network-motif logic.
column20: A.2 Structural Definitions: Network Motifs as Elementary Modules

column4: Steven S. Andrews, H. Steven Wiley, Herbert M. Sauro
column5: Design Patterns of Biological Cells
column9: Graph-based / network analysis / software engineering analogy
column10: Modules are implicitly defined through 'design patterns' — cataloged recurring solution templates for biological organization categorized as creational (processes building the cell), structural (topological layouts of reaction networks), and behavioral (reaction network functions including switching, adaptation, and oscillation). This constitutes an implicit functional-and-structural definition of biological modules organized by their recurring design solution roles. 21 patterns are identified across three categories.
column11: General cell biology, with applications to E. coli central metabolism and S. cerevisiae pheromone signaling. The paper is motivated by evolved biological systems and draws explicit analogies to software engineering design patterns.
column12: To catalog recurring solution templates (design patterns) in cell biology at a higher level of abstraction than individual network motifs, connecting network topology to biological function across diverse systems.
column13: No; the paper is a taxonomy and catalog without algorithmic implementation for pattern detection.
column14: No new experimental data; applies design pattern taxonomy to published E. coli and yeast network data as illustrative examples.
column16: multiple (E. coli, S. cerevisiae, general eukaryotes)
column17: Moderate impact. Provides a novel higher-level vocabulary for modular biological organization that bridges network motif analysis and functional descriptions, building explicitly on Alon's motif framework.
column19: This paper catalogs 21 design patterns in cell biology — creational, structural, and behavioral — as recurring solution templates for biological organization, providing a higher-level abstraction of modular function that connects network topology to biological purpose.

column4: Oguzhan Atay, Andreas Doncic, Jan M. Skotheim
column5: Switch-like Transitions Insulate Network Motifs to Modularize Biological Networks
column9: ODE / dynamical systems / experimental cell biology
column10: A module is a network motif or subnetwork that can be analyzed in functional isolation from the larger network. Insulation requires: (1) switch-like (ultrasensitive) behavior at the module boundary providing timescale separation, and (2) the amplitude of boundary signal change far exceeding prior gradual changes. Modules are dynamically and functionally defined — they are units whose behavior is independent of context because boundaries are insulated by switch-like transitions. Definition is both structural (the boundary interface) and functional (the switch character).
column11: Yeast pheromone signaling and cell cycle (G1/S transition) in S. cerevisiae. An evolved system in which a feedforward loop motif is embedded in the larger cell cycle network. The question is when a small motif can be validly analyzed in isolation.
column12: To show experimentally that switch-like (digital) transitions at network boundaries insulate embedded motifs from context, enabling valid modular analysis — and that disrupting switch character destroys modular behavior.
column13: Yes; experimental perturbations of the G1/S switch using synthetic constructs; quantitative fluorescence microscopy; mathematical modeling of feedforward loop behavior with and without switch insulation.
column14: Empirical data from yeast fluorescence microscopy with engineered perturbations of G1/S switch sharpness; quantitative measurement of motif behavior.
column17: High impact. Provides the biological mechanism for insulation that Del Vecchio formalized mathematically as retroactivity attenuation through timescale separation, bridging theory and experiment on when modular analysis is valid.
column19: This study provides experimental proof that switch-like transitions at network boundaries insulate embedded motifs from their context, enabling valid modular analysis, and that reducing switch character destroys modularity.

column4: Ziv Bar-Joseph, Georg K. Gerber, Tong I. Lee, Nicola J. Rinaldi, Jane Y. Yoo, François Robert, D. Benjamin Gordon, Ernest Fraenkel, Tommi S. Jaakkola, Richard A. Young, David K. Gifford
column5: Revealing modular organization in the yeast transcriptional network
column9: Graph-based / probabilistic model / gene expression analysis
column10: Transcription modules are context-dependent, potentially overlapping sets of genes regulated by shared transcription factors under specific conditions. Unlike traditional clusters, modules can overlap and are condition-dependent. Definition is structural and functional.
column11: Yeast transcriptional regulatory network. Modules identified by integrating genome-wide expression and TF binding data.
column12: To identify overlapping transcription modules revealing regulatory properties, cis-regulatory elements, and functional predictions for genes.
column13: Yes; probabilistic algorithm assigning genes to context-dependent, overlapping transcription modules by integrating expression and binding data.
column14: Genome-wide gene expression data and transcription factor-DNA binding data for S. cerevisiae.
column17: High impact (~600+ citations). Influential in transcription network analysis. Demonstrated importance of overlapping, condition-dependent modules.
column19: This paper proposes a method for identifying overlapping, context-dependent transcription modules in yeast by integrating expression and TF binding data, overcoming limitations of non-overlapping clustering.

column4: Alexander P. Clark, Mukti Chowkwale, Alexander Paap, Stephen Dang, Jeffrey J. Saucerman
column5: Logic-based modeling of biological networks with Netflux
column9: Logic-based / normalized-Hill ODE / Boolean-like network models
column10: Modules are not formally defined but the tool supports modular model construction: separate signaling sub-models can be built independently and then integrated. Modularity aids scientific discovery by enabling integration of separate models of complex biological functions.
column11: Signaling and gene-regulatory networks in mammalian cells, especially cardiovascular signaling. Models are constructed from literature-curated interactions and used for perturbation analysis.
column12: To facilitate modular construction and simulation of biological network models, enabling users to build, compose, and analyze signaling pathway models without programming.
column13: Yes; Netflux tool implements normalized-Hill differential equations for network simulation, perturbation analysis, and sensitivity analysis.
column14: No new experimental data; uses literature-curated network topologies. Tutorial demonstrates construction and simulation workflows.
column17: Moderate and growing. Netflux has been used in numerous published models over the past decade for cardiac signaling, fibrosis, and other cellular decision-making contexts.
column19: This paper describes Netflux, a user-friendly tool for constructing and simulating logic-based biological network models, demonstrating how modularity in model construction enables integration of separate signaling pathway models.

column4: Jeff Clune, Jean-Baptiste Mouret, Hod Lipson
column5: The Evolutionary Origins of Modularity
column9: Evolutionary simulation / neural networks / graph analysis
column10: Modules are defined implicitly as subnetworks shaped by selection to minimize inter-module connections while maintaining functional performance. Standard modularity metrics (Q-score) quantify modular organization. Modularity is a structural property of evolved networks reflecting the balance between intra-module cohesion and inter-module sparsity. The definition is structural rather than functional.
column11: Computational neural network evolution experiments, motivated by general biological network architecture. Selection is multi-objective: networks must solve a computational task and minimize connection costs (analogous to the cost of axons, regulatory interactions, or metabolic links).
column12: To demonstrate that minimizing connection costs — a universal selective pressure — is sufficient to drive spontaneous evolution of modular, evolvable networks, providing an alternative to Kashtan and Alon's modularly varying goals hypothesis.
column13: Yes; multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (NSGA-II) selecting for task performance and minimal connection cost; network modularity measured using Q-score.
column14: Computational simulation data from neural network evolution experiments.
column16: generic (computational model, applicable to gene networks, neural networks, metabolic networks)
column17: High impact (>600 citations). Shows that connection cost minimization — a more universal selective pressure — independently drives modularity, complementing Kashtan and Alon's hypothesis. Influential in computational evolution and systems biology.
column19: This study shows that networks evolved under multi-objective selection for performance and minimal connection costs spontaneously become modular and evolvable, offering a direct selective pressure explanation for the evolutionary origin of modularity.

column4: Michael Costanzo, Benjamin VanderSluis, Elizabeth N. Koch, Anastasia Baryshnikova, Carles Pons, Guihong Tan, Wen Wang, Matej Usaj, Julia Hanber, Chad L. Myers, et al.
column5: A global genetic interaction network maps a wiring diagram of cellular function
column9: Graph-based / genetic interaction networks / hierarchical clustering
column10: Modules are hierarchically organized groups of genes identified by genetic interaction profile similarity. Modules correspond to protein complexes, pathways, biological processes, and cellular compartments. Definition is both structural and functional. Modules can be nested.
column11: Genome-scale genetic interaction network in yeast. Over 23 million double mutants, ~900,000 interactions. Negative interactions connect functionally related genes; positive interactions map regulatory connections.
column12: To construct a hierarchical model of cellular function from genetic interactions, mapping functional modules from protein complexes to cellular processes.
column13: Yes; systematic double-mutant construction, genetic interaction scoring, hierarchical clustering, functional enrichment analysis.
column14: Massive genetic interaction dataset: ~23 million double mutants, ~900,000 genetic interactions in S. cerevisiae.
column17: Extremely high impact. One of the largest genetic interaction maps ever constructed. Widely used resource for functional genomics.
column19: This paper presents a genome-scale genetic interaction network for yeast from over 23 million double mutants, revealing a hierarchical model of cellular function organized into modules from protein complexes to biological processes.

column4: Michael Costanzo, Jing Hou, Vincent Messier, Justin Nelson, et al.
column5: A global genetic interaction map of a human cell reveals conserved principles of genetic networks
column9: Graph-based / genetic interaction networks / hierarchical clustering
column10: Modules are hierarchically organized gene groups identified from genetic interaction profiles in human cells. Modules correspond to protein complexes, pathways, biological processes, and compartments. Definition is structural and functional.
column11: Genome-scale genetic interaction network in human cells (HAP1). Maps ~90,000 genetic interactions from over 4 million double mutants. Shows conservation from yeast to human.
column12: To construct a hierarchical model of human cell function from genetic interactions and demonstrate conservation of modular organization from yeast to human.
column13: Yes; systematic double-mutant construction in human cells, genetic interaction profiling, hierarchical clustering, comparison with yeast network and DepMap.
column14: Large-scale genetic interaction data: ~4 million double mutants, ~90,000 genetic interactions in HAP1 human cells.
column17: High impact (very recent, 2025). Extends yeast genetic interaction framework to human cells, with cancer biology implications.
column19: This paper generates a genome-scale genetic interaction network in human cells, revealing hierarchical modular organization and demonstrating that genetic network principles are conserved from yeast to human.

column4: Ann E. Cowan, George W. Bhatt, Ivan Chang, et al.
column5: ModelBricks — Modules for Reproducible Modeling Improving Model Annotation and Provenance
column9: ODE / SBML / model annotation / database infrastructure
column10: Modules ('ModelBricks') are standardized, well-annotated, independently validated computational model components, each encapsulating a specific biological mechanism (e.g., a phosphorylation cycle, a feedback loop). Modules have defined interfaces specified by biological ontology terms, enabling automated composition. Definition is functional (each brick captures a specific mechanism) and engineering-inspired. Modules are non-overlapping by design; composition is via standardized interfaces.
column11: Computational modeling of biological systems, primarily intracellular signaling and gene regulatory networks. The context is model sharing and reproducibility in the systems biology community.
column12: To create a practical infrastructure for modular, reproducible computational model construction by developing a repository of standardized, annotated model components (ModelBricks) that can be composed into larger models.
column13: Yes; software platform (ModelBricks repository); automated model composition based on semantic annotation; model validation infrastructure.
column14: No new experimental data; demonstrates approach using existing computational models of biological mechanisms.
column17: Moderate impact. Important for the computational modeling community; provides practical infrastructure for the composable modeling philosophy advocated by Neal et al.
column19: This paper introduces ModelBricks, a platform for creating and sharing standardized, annotated modular computational model components that can be independently validated and composed into larger systems biology models.

column4: Crocker, J., et al.
column5: Cancer mutationscape: revealing the link between modular restructuring and intervention efficacy among mutations
column9: Boolean networks / Graph-based
column10: Structural. Defined as Strongly Connected Components (SCCs). Directed; can be nested in condensation graphs; non-trivial vs trivial nodes.
column11: Gene Regulatory Networks (GRNs) in cancer cells. Modules are used to identify topological impacts of mutations and intervention targets.
column12: To capture how mutations perturb modular structure (restructuring) and how this influences control/intervention efficacy.
column13: Yes; uses MATLAB "conncomp" and condensation graphs to identify SCC modules.
column14: Mutational data and network wiring diagrams.
column17: Links topological modularity directly to dynamical controllability in the context of cancer therapy.
column19: Analyzes how cancer mutations restructure the SCC-based modularity of GRNs and the resulting impact on intervention success.

column4: Marie E. Csete, John C. Doyle
column5: Reverse Engineering of Biological Complexity
column9: Control theory / network architecture / robustness theory
column10: Modules are hierarchically organized components within robust architectures. Modularity arises from the need for robustness. Both biological and engineered systems converge on modular architectures with protocols and layers of feedback regulation. Definition is structural and functional.
column11: General biological systems compared to engineered systems. Draws parallels between biological and technological complexity.
column12: To show that modular architecture in biology is a necessary consequence of the interplay between complexity, robustness, and fragility.
column13: No; conceptual review using engineering theory analogies.
column14: No new data; review drawing on examples from multiple biological and engineering systems.
column17: Very high impact (~2500+ citations). Influential in systems biology and control theory applied to biology.
column19: This review argues that biological and engineered systems share modular, hierarchical architectures driven by robustness needs, and that engineering control theory can illuminate the complexity-robustness-fragility trade-off in biology.

column4: Perla Del Conte-Zerial; Lutz Brusch; Jochen C. Rink; Claudio Collinet; Yannis Kalaidzidis; Marino Zerial; Andreas Deutsch
column5: Membrane identity and GTPase cascades regulated by toggle and cut-out switches
column9: ODE / dynamical systems / bifurcation analysis / modular systems model
column10: A module is defined explicitly as a subset of molecules that closely interact to jointly perform a specific function. Here each Rab module includes the GTPase together with regulators of its nucleotide cycle and its effectors. Modules are discrete but functionally interconnected, and they can be linked in cascades; the emphasis is functional and mechanistic rather than graph-partition based.
column11: Subcellular membrane trafficking in the endocytic pathway. The paper studies Rab5 and Rab7 modules controlling early-to-late endosome conversion and cargo progression, treating Rab domains as interconnected modules of membrane identity.
column12: To explain how mutually exclusive endosomal identities are generated and how cargo progresses from early to late endosomes. The module concept is used to uncover the design principle of the Rab5-to-Rab7 switch.
column13: yes - ODE model construction, screening of 54 candidate models, nullcline/phase-plane analysis, bifurcation analysis, numerical simulation, and statistical analysis of single-endosome tracking data
column14: Live-cell imaging and single-endosome tracking, fluorescence measurements of Rab5 and Rab7 densities, perturbation data on Rab mutants, and previously reported cell-biological/biochemical observations used for model testing.
column17: High impact in cell biology and systems biology: the paper makes modularity explicit for membrane trafficking and introduced the 'cut-out switch' as a new regulatory design principle for Rab conversion and organelle identity progression.
column19: The paper models Rab5 and Rab7 as interacting functional modules governing endosome maturation. Combining theory and live-cell imaging, it argues that Rab5-to-Rab7 conversion is better explained by a cut-out switch than by a classical toggle switch.
column20: A.2 Structural Definitions: Network Motifs as Elementary Modules

column4: Domitilla Del Vecchio, Alexander J. Ninfa, Eduardo D. Sontag
column5: Modular Cell Biology: Retroactivity and Insulation
column9: ODE / control theory / biochemical network analysis
column10: A module is a gene circuit component with defined input-output signal-response behavior. Retroactivity quantifies modularity failure: when a downstream module connects to an upstream one, it imposes a 'retroactivity to the input' that alters upstream behavior. Modules have defined boundaries (input/output signals) but these fail when inter-module molecular interactions are non-negligible. Insulators are proposed as mechanisms to restore modular behavior. Definition is functional and engineering-inspired.
column11: Natural and synthetic gene regulatory circuits. The core question is when small circuits can be analyzed in isolation and when interconnection invalidates such analysis.
column12: To quantify how connecting gene circuit modules causes retroactivity — a loading effect altering upstream dynamics — and to propose phosphorylation-cycle-based insulation devices restoring modular behavior.
column13: Yes; mathematical analysis of retroactivity using ODE models; derivation of conditions for large and small retroactivity; design of phosphorylation-cycle insulator with feedback amplification.
column14: No new experimental data; theoretical framework validated with analysis of published gene circuit models.
column16: generic (applicable to bacterial and eukaryotic gene circuits)
column17: Very high impact (>300 citations). Foundational paper introducing retroactivity as a formal, quantitative concept in biological modularity. Spawned a major research program on retroactivity measurement, insulation, and modular design in synthetic biology.
column19: This paper introduces retroactivity as a quantitative measure of modularity failure in gene circuits — the loading effect of downstream connections on upstream module dynamics — and proposes phosphorylation-cycle insulation devices to restore modular behavior.

column4: Domitilla Del Vecchio, Eduardo D. Sontag
column5: Engineering Principles in Bio-Molecular Systems: From Retroactivity to Modularity
column9: ODE / control theory / input-output systems analysis
column10: Modules are components in a biological input-output system with defined signal interfaces. The paper extends the retroactivity framework into a control-theoretic formalism: retroactivity is cast as a disturbance that must be rejected by an insulating device. Modules have well-defined input and output ports; modularity holds when input-output behavior of each component is preserved upon interconnection. Definition is functional and engineering-inspired.
column11: Engineered and natural biomolecular systems framed through control engineering analogies. Focus is on gene regulatory networks and signaling cascades as input-output systems.
column12: To expand retroactivity theory into a full control-theoretic input-output framework and cast retroactivity attenuation as a disturbance rejection problem, providing design principles for insulated modular biomolecular systems.
column13: No; theoretical framework paper extending Del Vecchio et al. (2008).
column14: No new experimental data; extends the theoretical analysis of Del Vecchio et al. (2008).
column17: Moderate impact. Extends retroactivity theory into a systems-and-control framework, important for synthetic biology and control engineering communities interested in modular biological design.
column19: This paper extends retroactivity theory into a control-theoretic input-output framework, casting retroactivity attenuation as a disturbance rejection problem and providing principles for designing modular biomolecular systems.

column4: Domitilla Del Vecchio
column5: Modularity, Context-Dependence, and Insulation in Engineered Biological Circuits
column9: ODE / control theory / synthetic biology
column10: A module is an engineered biological circuit component with context-independent input-output behavior. The review identifies three sources of context-dependence undermining modularity: retroactivity (loading at interconnection points), resource competition (shared transcriptional/translational machinery), and growth-rate coupling. Insulation strategies are reviewed for each. Definition is functional and engineering-inspired; modularity is a property that must be actively engineered, not assumed.
column11: Engineered synthetic gene circuits primarily in E. coli and S. cerevisiae. Synthesizes a decade of work on achieving reliable modular design in synthetic biology.
column12: To synthesize understanding of how retroactivity, resource competition, and growth-rate coupling cause context-dependence in engineered biological circuits, and to review design strategies restoring modular behavior.
column13: No; review paper synthesizing existing theoretical and experimental work.
column14: No new experimental data; reviews published experimental and theoretical studies on retroactivity, resource competition, and growth coupling.
column16: multiple (E. coli, S. cerevisiae, general synthetic biology)
column17: High impact. Capstone review of a decade of retroactivity and insulation research. Important reference for synthetic biology community on achieving reliable modular design.
column19: This capstone review synthesizes three sources of context-dependence in engineered biological circuits — retroactivity, resource competition, and growth coupling — and reviews design strategies to achieve reliable modularity.

column4: Dávid Deritei, William C. Aird, Mária Ercsey-Ravasz & Erzsébet Ravasz Regan
column5: Principles of dynamical modularity in biological regulatory networks
column9: Dyamical systems
column10: Dynamical modules are robust regulatory switch—i.e., a multistable subnetwork—whose stable dynamical states correspond to discrete, biologically meaningful phenotypes.
column11: Regulatory systems

column4: Deritei, D., et al.
column5: Principles of dynamical modularity in biological regulatory networks
column9: Boolean networks / Network science
column10: Dynamical. Modules are discrete functional parts identified by their ability to function somewhat independently within a larger dynamical system.
column11: Regulatory networks. Focus on how malfunctions (diseases) arise from modular failures. Bottom-up view.
column12: Capturing discrete circuits that provide robust biological functions.
column13: Yes; specific dynamical analysis tools for Boolean models.
column14: Regulatory interaction data.
column17: Introduced "dynamical modularity" as a distinct concept from static structural modularity.
column19: Explores how biological networks form discrete dynamical modules and how these small circuits function within the global system.

column4: Brendan Fong, David Spivak
column5: Structured versus Decorated Cospans
column9: Category theory / monoidal double categories
column10: Modules are not defined in the biological sense. The paper develops mathematical frameworks (structured cospans and decorated cospans) for compositional reasoning about systems. If modularity implies compositionality, these frameworks provide tools for studying how subsystem behaviors compose.
column11: General mathematical framework applicable to graphs, circuits, Petri nets, reaction networks, and dynamical systems.
column12: To develop general mathematical frameworks for compositional modeling where complex systems are understood through interconnected subsystems.
column13: No; purely theoretical mathematical framework.
column14: No data; mathematical theory paper.
column17: Moderate impact in applied category theory. Foundational for compositional approaches to systems modeling.
column19: This paper develops and compares two general mathematical frameworks—structured cospans and decorated cospans—for studying systems compositionally, with applications to graphs, circuits, Petri nets, and dynamical systems.

column4: Tamar Friedlander, Avraham E. Mayo, Tsvi Tlusty, Uri Alon
column5: Evolution of Bow-Tie Architectures in Biology
column9: Graph-based / evolutionary simulation / network architecture
column10: Modules are components of bow-tie architectures: fan-in layers, a core (conserved processing layer), and fan-out layers. The bow-tie core acts as a universal interface between input and output modules.
column11: Biological systems at multiple levels. Bow-tie architectures are products of evolution emerging under constraints of modularity and robustness.
column12: To explain how and why bow-tie architectures evolve in biology, showing they emerge from evolutionary optimization and enable modular reuse of core components.
column13: Yes; evolutionary simulations of network architecture under varying selection pressures.
column14: Computational simulation data; analysis of known biological bow-tie architectures.
column17: Moderate to high. Provides evolutionary theory for a commonly observed modular network architecture.
column19: This paper uses evolutionary simulations to show that bow-tie architectures evolve naturally under changing environments and provide a universal interface between diverse input and output modules.

column4: Elisa Gallo, Stefano De Renzis, James Sharpe, Roberto Mayor, Jonas Hartmann
column5: Versatile System Cores as a Conceptual Basis for Generality in Cell and Developmental Biology
column9: General (conceptual framework / developmental biology)
column10: A 'versatile system core' is a conserved molecular module reused across diverse cellular and developmental contexts by being embedded in different regulatory environments. The same core module can produce qualitatively different behaviors depending on context — context-dependence is a feature of versatile cores, not a failure of modularity. This reframes the tension between modularity and context-dependence: cores are modular (structurally conserved, reusable) but functionally versatile. Definition is structural (conserved molecular assembly) and functional (context-dependent behavior).
column11: Cell and developmental biology broadly. Examples span morphogenetic processes, signaling pathways, and gene regulatory circuits. The context is evolved developmental systems where the same molecular modules are repurposed across contexts.
column12: To propose 'versatile system cores' as a framework for understanding how the same molecular modules underlie diverse biological phenomena, reframing context-dependence as a key feature rather than a problem for modularity.
column13: No; conceptual perspective.
column14: No new experimental data; draws on published examples from cell and developmental biology.
column17: Emerging impact (recent, 2024). Provides a conceptual reframing of the modularity-context-dependence tension applicable across cell and developmental biology.
column19: This perspective introduces 'versatile system cores' — conserved molecular modules producing diverse biological behaviors depending on regulatory context — reframing context-dependence as a key feature of reusable modular components rather than a failure of modularity.

column4: Girvan, M., Newman, M. E. J.
column5: Community structure in social and biological networks
column9: Graph theory / Community detection
column10: Structural. "Communities" defined as groups of nodes with high density of internal edges. Non-overlapping (in original algorithm); hierarchical through clustering.
column11: Social, biological (food webs, neural networks). Modules identify functional subdivisions in large data sets.
column12: To understand the interplay between network structure and global function by identifying "tightly knit groups."
column13: Yes; Edge betweenness-based community detection algorithm.
column14: Real-world networks (C. elegans neural net, food webs).

column19: This is a seminal paper on complex systems. It claims that all complex systems have many shared properties. It is broken up into four parts: (1) an observation that complex systems tend to take the form of hierarchy, the complex system being composed of subsystems that, in turn, have their own subsystems, and so on; (2) an argument that hierarchic systems will evolve far more quickly than non-hierarchic systems of comparable size; (3) the dynamic properties of hierarchically-organized systems, can be decomposed into subsystems in order 65to analyze their behavior; (4) there is a relationship between complex system structures and their behaviors.

Not explicitly graph-based; not directed or undirected; modules can be nested but do not share elements.
column11: General complex systems spanning biological, social, and physical domains. Not specific to a single biological system; uses cells, organs, organisms, and ecosystems as illustrative examples. Theoretical essay on evolved and engineered systems alike. Purpose is conceptual analysis of why complexity takes hierarchical modular form.
column12: To explain why complex systems evolve and persist as hierarchical, nearly decomposable structures. The watchmaker parable argues modular architecture accelerates evolution by enabling stable intermediate forms. Modularity enables independent development and adaptation of subsystems and reduces the combinatorial complexity of system assembly.
column13: No. Purely theoretical/conceptual; no algorithm or computational implementation is provided.
column14: No empirical data. Uses thought experiments (the watchmaker parable, the Hora and Tempus parable) and illustrative physical examples (thermal equilibration in near-decomposable systems, hierarchy in atomic/molecular structure).
column15: Not specific; spans molecular, cellular, organismal, social, and physical systems as illustrative examples.
column17: Foundational and extraordinarily influential across all disciplines studying complexity. Introduced "near-decomposability" and hierarchical modularity as core organizing principles. Widely cited in systems biology, computer science, economics, organizational theory, and engineering. One of the most-cited papers on modularity and complexity in the scientific literature.
column19: This foundational essay argues that complex systems in nature and society share a common hierarchical, nearly decomposable architecture in which strong within-module interactions and weak between-module interactions enable rapid evolution and stable adaptive behavior.

column4: Mark E. J. Newman
column5: Modularity and community structure in networks
column9: Graph-based; spectral graph theory; modularity matrix eigenvalue decomposition
column10: A module (community) is a densely connected group of vertices in a network with only sparser connections between groups. Undirected; non-hierarchical (flat partition of the network); non-overlapping (each node belongs to exactly one community). Purely structural/graph-based definition formalized through the modularity quality function Q, which compares the fraction of intra-community edges to that expected in a random graph with the same degree sequence. Modules cannot share elements or be nested in the basic formulation.
column11: General network analysis applicable to social networks, biological networks (metabolic, regulatory), computer networks, and ecological networks. Not specific to one biological setting. Demonstrated on published empirical datasets including social networks and ecological networks. Purpose is community detection methodology.
column12: To detect and characterize community structure (dense subgroups) in complex networks. In biological applications, identified communities correspond to putative functional units in metabolic or regulatory networks. The modularity Q-score quantifies the quality of any proposed partition.
column13: Yes; introduces a spectral algorithm based on the leading eigenvector of the modularity matrix B, where B_ij = A_ij - (k_i * k_j) / 2m. The algorithm partitions networks by iterative bisection: at each step, the leading eigenvector of B is computed and nodes are assigned to two groups based on the sign of their eigenvector components, maximizing the modularity Q.
column14: Uses several published benchmark network datasets for validation: Zachary's karate club, a dolphin social network, NCAA college football scheduling, and a political books co-purchasing network. All are literature-curated empirical datasets.
column15: Not specific to a biological level; the framework is general and domain-agnostic.
column16: None specific (general method demonstrated on social and ecological datasets)
column17: Extremely high impact. One of the most influential papers in network science. The modularity matrix and spectral partitioning approach became standard tools across physics, biology, social science, and computer science. Widely adopted in systems biology for identifying functional modules in gene regulatory, metabolic, and protein-protein interaction networks.
column19: This paper reformulates the network modularity quality function Q in terms of eigenvectors of a modularity matrix, yielding a spectral algorithm for community detection that outperforms competing methods in quality and speed across several published network datasets.

column4: Claus Kadelka, Matthew Wheeler, Alan Veliz-Cuba, David Murrugarra, Reinhard Laubenbacher
column5: Modularity of biological systems: a link between structure and function
column9: Boolean network; algebraic decomposition theory (direct product and semidirect product of Boolean networks); graph-based (wiring diagram / dependency graph)
column10: A module is a strongly connected component (SCC) of the wiring diagram (dependency graph) of a Boolean network such that the full network decomposes as an iterated semidirect product of sub-networks over these components. Directed; hierarchical (the condensation DAG induces a partial order on modules); non-overlapping (each node belongs to exactly one SCC). The definition explicitly bridges structural and functional perspectives: the structural SCC decomposition induces an analogous decomposition of the state transition graph (dynamics). Not based on density/community detection; based on algebraic decomposition theory.
column11: Gene regulatory networks modeled as Boolean networks. Applies to both random Boolean networks with controlled modularity parameters and published biological Boolean network models. The focus is on understanding how modular network structure relates to dynamical function and whether modularity confers evolutionary advantages. Evolved systems; analysis and theoretical validation.
column12: To establish a rigorous mathematical link between structural modularity (SCC decomposition of the wiring diagram) and functional modularity (decomposition of the state transition graph). Additionally, to provide simulation evidence that modularity may have evolved to increase phenotypic complexity while maintaining maximal dynamic robustness to external perturbations.
column13: Yes; modules are identified via strongly connected component decomposition of the wiring diagram, followed by algebraic decomposition of the Boolean network into an iterated semidirect product of component sub-networks. The paper provides constructive algorithms for this decomposition and for computing the induced dynamics.
column14: Simulated data from ensembles of random Boolean networks with controlled modularity parameters (varying number, size, and connectivity of modules). Also applies the framework to published biological Boolean network models curated from the literature. No new empirical biological data.
column16: Not organism-specific; uses published Boolean network models of various biological systems
column17: Moderate and growing. Provides the first rigorous mathematical framework formally connecting structural and functional modularity in Boolean networks. Bridges graph-theoretic (SCC) and algebraic (semidirect product) approaches. The simulation-based evolutionary hypothesis (modularity increases phenotypic complexity while maintaining robustness) contributes to the broader debate on the evolution of modularity.
column19: This paper establishes a mathematical correspondence between structural modularity (SCC decomposition of the wiring diagram) and functional modularity (state-space decomposition) in Boolean networks, and provides simulation evidence that modularity may have evolved to increase phenotypic complexity while preserving dynamic robustness.
